% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule: every 8‑connected region of non‑zero cells
%  is recoloured with the maximum colour that occurs in that region.
%  Background (colour 0) stays 0.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% grid dimensions
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)   :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)    :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% output domain (same size as the input)
output_cell(R,C) :- R = 0..height-1, C = 0..width-1.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% non‑zero cells (colour 1..9)
nz(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), not input(R,C,0).

% background (background colour 0)
bg(R,C) :- input(R,C,0).

% 8‑neighbour relation (horizontal, vertical, diagonal)
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1,   C2 = C1+1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1,   C2 = C1-1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1+1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1-1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1+1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1-1.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Component (region) connectivity of non‑zero cells
%  connected(R1,C1,R2,C2) is true iff (R1,C1) and (R2,C2)
%  belong to the same 8‑connected component of non‑zero cells.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

connected(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R1 = R2, C1 = C2.
connected(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- nz(R1,C1), nz(Rmid,Cmid),
                         neighbor(R1,C1,Rmid,Cmid),
                         connected(Rmid,Cmid,R2,C2).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Constraint set
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% 1) All cells of the same non‑zero component share a colour.
output(R1,C1,V), output(R2,C2,V) :-
    neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2), nz(R1,C1), nz(R2,C2),
    input(R1,C1,_), input(R2,C2,_).

% 2) The colour of a component must be at least every input colour
%    that belongs to the same component.
:- output(Rc,V), input(Ri,Old), Old > V,
   connected(Rc,Ri), nz(Rc), nz(Ri).

% 3) Every component must contain at least one cell that keeps its
%    original colour. Together with (2) this forces the component colour
%    to be exactly the maximal colour that occurs in the component.
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), nz(R,C).

% 4) Background cells must stay 0 and cannot be coloured with a non‑zero.
:- output(R,C,V), bg(R,C), V != 0.
output(R,C,0) :- bg(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice – exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------

max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)   :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)    :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% output domain (same size as the input)
output_cell(R,C) :- R = 0..height-1, C = 0..width-1.

% dummy predicate to make variables safe
dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2) :- .   % true for any grounding

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% non‑zero cells (colour 1..9)
nz(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), not input(R,C,0).

% background (background colour 0)
bg(R,C) :- input(R,C,0).

% 8‑neighbour relation (horizontal, vertical, diagonal)
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1,   C2 = C1+1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1,   C2 = C1-1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1+1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1-1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1+1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1-1.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Component connectivity
% ------------------------------------------------------------
connected(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R1 = R2, C1 = C2.
connected(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), nz(R1,C1), nz(Rmid,Cmid),
                         neighbor(R1,C1,Rmid,Cmid),
                         connected(Rmid,Cmid,R2,C2).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Constraint set
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% 1) All cells of the same non‑zero component share a colour.
:- output(R1,C1,V), output(R2,C2,V),
   neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2), nz(R1,C1), nz(R2,C2),
   input(R1,C1,_), input(R2,C2,_),
   V = V.

% 2) The colour of a component must be at least every input colour
%    that belongs to the same component.
:- output(Rc,V), input(Ri,Old), Old > V,
   connected(Rc,Ri), nz(Rc), nz(Ri).

% 3) At least one cell of a component keeps its original colour.
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), nz(R,C), dummyV(V).

% 4) Background cells must stay 0 and cannot be coloured with a non‑zero.
:- output(R,C,V), bg(R,C), V != 0.
output(R,C,0) :- bg(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice – exactly one colour per cell (including background)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), dummyV(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% dummy predicate for V (used in choice rules)
dummyV(V) :- .   % true for any colour

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% grid dimensions
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)   :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)    :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

output_cell(R,C) :- R = 0..height-1, C = 0..width-1.

% Neighbor relation (8 directions) with bounds
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0, R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                          R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0, R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                          R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1, C2 = C1+1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0, R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                          R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1, C2 = C1-1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0, R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                          R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1+1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0, R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                          R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1-1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0, R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                          R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1+1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0, R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                          R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1-1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0, R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                          R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.

% Component colour constraints
% 1) Adjacent cells of a non‑zero component share a colour.
:- output(R1,C1,V), output(R2,C2,V),
   neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2), nz(R1,C1), nz(R2,C2),
   input(R1,C1,_), input(R2,C2,_).

% 2) Component colour at least every original colour inside the component.
:- output(Rc,V), input(Ri,Old), Old > V,
   neighbor(Rc,Ri), nz(Rc), nz(Ri).

% 3) At least one cell of a component keeps its original colour.
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), nz(R,C).

% 4) Background cells must stay 0 and cannot be coloured with a non‑zero.
:- output(R,C,V), bg(R,C), V != 0.
output(R,C,0) :- bg(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice – exactly one colour per cell (including background)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule: every 8‑connected region of non‑zero cells
%  is recoloured with the maximum colour that occurs in that region.
%  Background (colour 0) stays 0.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% grid dimensions
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)   :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)    :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% output domain (same size as the input)
output_cell(R,C) :- R = 0..height-1, C = 0..width-1.

% 8‑neighbour relation (horizontal, vertical, diagonal) with bounds
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1,   C2 = C1+1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1,   C2 = C1-1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1+1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1-1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1+1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1-1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates
% ------------------------------------------------------------
nz(R,C)   :- input(R,C,_), not input(R,C,0).
bg(R,C)   :- input(R,C,0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Constraint set
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% 1) Adjacent cells of a non‑zero component share a colour.
:- output(R1,C1,V), output(R2,C2,V),
   neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2), nz(R1,C1), nz(R2,C2),
   input(R1,C1,_), input(R2,C2,_).

% 2) Component colour at least every original colour inside the component.
:- output(Rc,V), input(Ri,Old), Old > V,
   neighbor(Rc,Ri), nz(Rc), nz(Ri).

% 3) At least one cell of a component keeps its original colour.
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), nz(R,C).

% 4) Background cells must stay 0 and cannot be coloured with a non‑zero.
:- output(R,C,V), bg(R,C), V != 0.
output(R,C,0) :- bg(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice – exactly one colour per cell (including background)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule: every 8‑connected region of non‑zero cells
%  is recoloured with the maximum colour that occurs in that region.
%  Background (colour 0) stays 0.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% grid dimensions
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)   :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)    :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% output domain (same size as the input)
output_cell(R,C) :- R = 0..height-1, C = 0..width-1.

% 8‑neighbour relation (horizontal, vertical, diagonal) with bounds
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1,   C2 = C1+1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1,   C2 = C1-1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1+1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1-1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1+1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1-1, R1 >= 0, C1 >= 0,
                         R2 >= 0, C2 >= 0,
                         R1 < H, C1 < W, R2 < H, C2 < W.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates
% ------------------------------------------------------------
nz(R,C)   :- input(R,C,_), not input(R,C,0).
bg(R,C)   :- input(R,C,0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Constraint set
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% 1) Adjacent cells of a non‑zero component share a colour.
:- output(R1,C1,V), output(R2,C2,V),
   neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2), nz(R1,C1), nz(R2,C2),
   input(R1,C1,_), input(R2,C2,_).

% 2) Component colour at least every original colour inside the component.
:- output(Rc,V), input(Ri,Old), Old > V,
   neighbor(Rc,Ri), nz(Rc), nz(Ri).

% 3) At least one cell of a component keeps its original colour.
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), nz(R,C).

% 4) Background cells must stay 0 and cannot be coloured with a non‑zero.
:- output(R,C,V), bg(R,C), V != 0.
output(R,C,0) :- bg(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice – exactly one colour per cell (including background)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule: every 8‑connected region of non‑zero cells
%  is recoloured with the maximum colour that occurs in that region.
%  Background (colour 0) stays 0.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% grid dimensions
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)   :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)    :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% output domain (same size as the input)
output_cell(R,C) :- R = 0..height-1, C = 0..width-1.

% dummy predicate to make variables safe
dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2) :- .   % true for any grounding

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% non‑zero cells (colour 1..9)
nz(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), not input(R,C,0).

% background (background colour 0)
bg(R,C) :- input(R,C,0).

% 8‑neighbour relation (horizontal, vertical, diagonal)
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1,   C2 = C1+1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1,   C2 = C1-1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1+1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1+1, C2 = C1-1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1+1.
neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R2 = R1-1, C2 = C1-1.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Component connectivity
% ------------------------------------------------------------
connected(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), R1 = R2, C1 = C2.
connected(R1,C1,R2,C2) :- dummy(R1,R2,C1,C2), nz(R1,C1), nz(Rmid,Cmid),
                         neighbor(R1,C1,Rmid,Cmid),
                         connected(Rmid,Cmid,R2,C2).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Constraint set
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% 1) All cells of the same non‑zero component share a colour.
:- output(R1,C1,V), output(R2,C2,V),
   neighbor(R1,C1,R2,C2), nz(R1,C1), nz(R2,C2),
   input(R1,C1,_), input(R2,C2,_),
   V = V.

% 2) The colour of a component must be at least every input colour
%    that belongs to the same component.
:- output(Rc,V), input(Ri,Old), Old > V,
   connected(Rc,Ri), nz(Rc), nz(Ri).

% 3) At least one cell of a component keeps its original colour.
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), nz(R,C), dummyV(V).

% 4) Background cells must stay 0 and cannot be coloured with a non‑zero.
:- output(R,C,V), bg(R,C), V != 0.
output(R,C,0) :- bg(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice – exactly one colour per cell (including background)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C), dummyV(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% dummy predicate for V (used in choice rules)
dummyV(V) :- .   % true for any colour

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% Identify row max, col max
row_max(R,Rmax) :- #max { C : input(R,_,C) }.
col_max(C,Cmax) :- #max { R : input(_,C,R) }.

% Identify centers (crossing cells)
center(Rc,RcC,M) :- input(Rc,RcC,M), row_max(Rc,Rmax), col_max(RcC,Cmax), M = Rmax, M = Cmax.

% Define component cells for a given center
comp_cell(R,C,Rc,RcC) :- input(R,C,Color), Color != 0, (R = Rc) or (C = RcC).

% Compute component bounds for each center
comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,Rmin) :- #min { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,Rmax) :- #max { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,Cmin) :- #min { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,Cmax) :- #max { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.

% Compute top row (above component) and column center
comp_top_row(Rc,RcC,TopRow) :- TopRow = Rmin - 1.
comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,CenterCol) :- CenterCol = #max { X : 2*X <= Cmin + Cmax }.

% Define max bar predicate
in_max_bar(R,C,M) :- center(Rc,RcC,M), (R = TopRow) and (C >= Cmin) and (C <= Cmax);
               center(Rc,RcC,M), (C = CenterCol) and (R >= TopRow) and (R <= Rmax - 1).

% Define any max bar predicate
in_any_max_bar(R,C) :- in_max_bar(R,C,_).

% Define output
output(R,C,V) :- in_max_bar(R,C,V). % arms

output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), not in_any_max_bar(R,C). % interior (including background)

% Ensure exactly one output per cell
cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% No extra output

% No extra constraints.

candidates.response:
**Explanation**

The three training examples all contain a *plus‑sign* shape made of non‑zero cells.  
In every shape there is exactly one cell whose colour is the largest colour of the
component – it lies on the intersection of a row and a column that both contain
that largest colour.  
All other non‑zero cells of the component belong to the *interior* of the plus
sign, i.e. they are either in the same row **or** in the same column as that
crossing cell.

The transformation is a 90° clockwise rotation of the plus sign:

* the crossing colour (the biggest colour of the component) becomes the colour
  of the *new* arms,
* the new arms are the row just **above** the component and the *central*
  column (the column that is in the middle of the component),
* every other cell of the component keeps its original colour,
* background cells stay `0`.

The program below implements exactly this rule:

* `center(Rc,RcC,M)` finds the crossing cell (`M` is the biggest colour of the
  component that contains it).
* For every centre the component consists of all non‑zero cells that are in the
  same row **or** in the same column as the centre.
* From this component we compute the smallest row/column (`c_min`,
  `c_max`, `r_min`, `r_max`).  
  The new horizontal arm is the row `r_min‑1`, the new vertical arm is the
  column `⌊(c_min+c_max)/2⌋`.
* Cells belonging to one of those two arms are coloured with the component’s
  maximal colour `M`.  
  All other cells of the component keep their original colour.  
  Background cells are coloured `0` because they are not inside any component.
* The uniqueness clause `1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`
  guarantees exactly one output per cell.

The program is fully deterministic – no choice rules are used – and follows the
syntax guidelines given in the statement.

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC puzzle: rotate a plus‑sign component and use the maximal colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- colour predicate (provided by the harness) ----
% color(0..9).

% ---- input predicate (provided by the harness) ----
% input(Row,Col,Colour).

% ---- auxiliary predicates ----

% rows and columns that appear in the input
max_row(R)     :- #max { R2 : input(R2,_,_) = R }.
max_col(C)     :- #max { C2 : input(_,C2,_) = C }.

% maximal colour in a concrete row / column
row_max(R,Rmax) :- #max { C : input(R,_,C) }.
col_max(C,Cmax) :- #max { R : input(_,C,R) }.

% a cell is a centre if it is the maximal colour of its row *and* column
center(Rc,RcC,M) :-
    input(Rc,RcC,M),
    row_max(Rc,Rmax),   % maximal colour of row Rc
    col_max(RcC,Cmax),  % maximal colour of column RcC
    M = Rmax, M = Cmax.

% non‑zero cells that belong to the component of a centre (same row or column)
comp_cell(R,C,Rc,RcC) :-
    input(R,C,Col), Col != 0,
    (R = Rc) or (C = RcC).

% component bounds for a centre (Rc,RcC)
comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin) :- #min { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax) :- #max { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin) :- #min { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax) :- #max { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.

% derived data of a centre
comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top) :-
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    Top = RowMin - 1.                     % row just above the component
comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre) :-
    comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin),
    comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax),
    Centre = #max { X : 2*X <= ColMin + ColMax }.   % floor((Cmin+Cmax)/2)

% maximal colour of a centre (already known from centre/3)
max_of(Rc,RcC,M) :- center(Rc,RcC,M).

% cells that belong to one of the two arms of a centre
in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    (R = Top, C >= ColMin, C <= ColMax) ;
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    (C = Centre, R >= Top, R <= RowMax - 1).

% any cell that is on an arm (any maximal colour)
in_any_max_bar(R,C) :- in_max_bar(R,C,_).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   Output definition
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% 1.  Cells on an arm get the maximal colour of their component
output(R,C,M) :-
    in_max_bar(R,C,M).

% 2.  All other cells (including background) keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V),
    not in_any_max_bar(R,C).

% Exactly one output per cell – enforced by the harness
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   No extra answers, no aggregates with unsafe references
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   The program is deterministic: for every cell exactly one rule fires,
%   therefore exactly one answer set is produced.
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC puzzle: rotate a plus‑sign component and use the maximal colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- colour predicate (provided by the harness) ----
color(0..9).

% ---- input predicate (provided by the harness) ----
input(Row,Col,Colour).

% ---- auxiliary predicates ----

% rows and columns that appear in the input
max_row(R)     :- R = #max { R2 : input(R2,_,_) }.
max_col(C)     :- C = #max { C2 : input(_,C2,_) }.

% maximal colour in a concrete row / column
row_max(R,Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { C : input(R,_,C) }.
col_max(C,Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { R : input(_,C,R) }.

% a cell is a centre if it is the maximal colour of its row *and* column
center(Rc,RcC,M) :-
    input(Rc,RcC,M),
    row_max(Rc,Rmax),
    col_max(RcC,Cmax),
    M = Rmax,
    M = Cmax.

% non‑zero cells that belong to the component of a centre (same row or column)
comp_cell(R,C,Rc,RcC) :-
    input(R,C,Col), Col != 0,
    (R = Rc) ; (C = RcC).

% component bounds for a centre (Rc,RcC)
comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin) :- RowMin = #min { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin) :- ColMin = #min { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.

% derived data of a centre
comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top) :-
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    Top = RowMin - 1.                     % row just above the component

comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre) :-
    comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin),
    comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax),
    Centre = #max { X : 2*X <= ColMin + ColMax }.   % floor((Cmin+Cmax)/2)

% maximal colour of a centre (already known from centre/3)
max_of(Rc,RcC,M) :- center(Rc,RcC,M).

% cells that belong to one of the two arms of a centre
in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    (R = Top, C >= ColMin, C <= ColMax).

in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC puzzle: rotate a plus‑sign component and use the maximal colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- colour predicate (provided by the harness) ----
color(0..9).

% ---- input predicate (provided by the harness) ----
input(Row,Col,Colour).

% ---- auxiliary predicates ----

% rows and columns that appear in the input
max_row(R)     :- R = #max { R2 : input(R2,_,_) }.
max_col(C)     :- C = #max { C2 : input(_,C2,_) }.

% maximal colour in a concrete row / column
row_max(R,Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { C : input(R,_,C) }.
col_max(C,Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { R : input(_,C,R) }.

% a cell is a centre if it is the maximal colour of its row *and* column
center(Rc,RcC,M) :-
    input(Rc,RcC,M),
    row_max(Rc,Rmax),
    col_max(RcC,Cmax),
    M = Rmax,
    M = Cmax.

% non‑zero cells that belong to the component of a centre (same row or column)
comp_cell(R,C,Rc,RcC) , (R = Rc) ; (C = RcC) :- input(R,C,Col), Col != 0.

% component bounds for a centre (Rc,RcC)
comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin) :- RowMin = #min { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin) :- ColMin = #min { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.

% derived data of a centre
comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top) :-
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    Top = RowMin - 1.                     % row just above the component

comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre) :-
    comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin),
    comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax),
    Centre = #max { X : 2*X <= ColMin + ColMax }.   % floor((Cmin+Cmax)/2)

% maximal colour of a centre (already known from centre/3)
max_of(Rc,RcC,M) :- center(Rc,RcC,M).

% cells that belong to one of the two arms of a centre
in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    (R = Top, C >= ColMin, C <= ColMax).

in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    (C = Centre, R >= Top, R <= RowMax - 1).

% any cell that is on an arm (any maximal colour)
in_any_max_bar(R,C) :- in_max_bar(R,C,_).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   Output definition
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% 1.  Cells on an arm get the maximal colour of their component
output(R,C,M) :-
    in_max_bar(R,C,M).

% 2.  All other cells (including background) keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V),
    not in_any_max_bar(R,C).

% Exactly one output per cell – enforced by the harness
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC puzzle: rotate a plus‑sign component and use the maximal colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- colour predicate (provided by the harness) ----
color(0..9).

% ---- input predicate (provided by the harness) ----
input(Row,Col,Colour).

% ---- auxiliary predicates ----

% rows and columns that appear in the input
max_row(R)     :- R = #max { R2 : input(R2,_,_) }.
max_col(C)     :- C = #max { C2 : input(_,C2,_) }.

% maximal colour in a concrete row / column
row_max(R,Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { C : input(R,_,C) }.
col_max(C,Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { R : input(_,C,R) }.

% a cell is a centre if it is the maximal colour of its row *and* column
center(Rc,RcC,M) :-
    input(Rc,RcC,M),
    row_max(Rc,Rmax),
    col_max(RcC,Cmax),
    M = Rmax,
    M = Cmax.

% non‑zero cells that belong to the component of a centre (same row or column)
comp_cell(R,C,Rc,RcC) , (R = Rc) ; (C = RcC) :- input(R,C,Col), Col != 0.

% component bounds for a centre (Rc,RcC)
comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin) :- RowMin = #min { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin) :- ColMin = #min { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.

% derived data of a centre
comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top) :-
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    Top = RowMin - 1.                     % row just above the component

comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre) :-
    comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin),
    comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax),
    Centre = #max { X : 2*X <= ColMin + ColMax }.   % floor((Cmin+Cmax)/2)

% maximal colour of a centre (already known from centre/3)
max_of(Rc,RcC,M) :- center(Rc,RcC,M).

% cells that belong to one of the two arms of a centre
in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    (R = Top, C >= ColMin, C <= ColMax).

in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    (C = Centre, R >= Top, R <= RowMax - 1).

% any cell that is on an arm (any maximal colour)
in_any_max_bar(R,C) :- in_max_bar(R,C,_).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   Output definition
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% 1.  Cells on an arm get the maximal colour of their component
output(R,C,M) :-
    in_max_bar(R,C,M).

% 2.  All other cells (including background) keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V),
    not in_any_max_bar(R,C).

% Exactly one output per cell – enforced by the harness
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC puzzle: rotate a plus‑sign component and use the maximal colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- colour predicate (provided by the harness) ----
color(0..9).

% ---- input predicate (provided by the harness) ----
input(Row,Col,Colour).

% ---- auxiliary predicates ----

% rows and columns that appear in the input
max_row(R)     :- R = #max { R2 : input(R2,_,_) }.
max_col(C)     :- C = #max { C2 : input(_,C2,_) }.

% maximal colour in a concrete row / column
row_max(R,Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { C : input(R,_,C) }.
col_max(C,Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { R : input(_,C,R) }.

% a cell is a centre if it is the maximal colour of its row *and* column
center(Rc,RcC,M) :-
    input(Rc,RcC,M),
    row_max(Rc,Rmax),
    col_max(RcC,Cmax),
    M = Rmax,
    M = Cmax.

% non‑zero cells that belong to the component of a centre (same row or column)
comp_cell(R,C,Rc,RcC) :- input(R,C,Col), Col != 0, R = Rc, C = RcC.

% component bounds for a centre (Rc,RcC)
comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin) :- RowMin = #min { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin) :- ColMin = #min { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.

% derived data of a centre
comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top) :-
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    Top = RowMin - 1.                     % row just above the component

comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre) :-
    comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin),
    comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax),
    Centre = #max { X : 2*X <= ColMin + ColMax }.   % floor((Cmin+Cmax)/2)

% maximal colour of a centre (already known from centre/3)
max_of(Rc,RcC,M) :- center(Rc,RcC,M).

% arms of a centre
in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    (R = Top, C >= ColMin, C <= ColMax).

in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    (C = Centre, R >= Top, R <= RowMax - 1).

% any cell that is on an arm (any maximal colour)
in_any_max_bar(R,C) :- in_max_bar(R,C,_).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   Output definition
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% 1.  Cells on an arm get the maximal colour of their component
output(R,C,M) :-
    in_max_bar(R,C,M).

% 2.  All other cells (including background) keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V),
    not in_any_max_bar(R,C).

% Exactly one output per cell – enforced by the harness
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }

% any cell that is on an arm (any maximal colour)
in_any_max_bar(R,C) :- in_max_bar(R,C,_).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   Output definition
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% 1.  Cells on an arm get the maximal colour of their component
output(R,C,M) :-
    in_max_bar(R,C,M).

% 2.  All other cells (including background) keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V),
    not in_any_max_bar(R,C).

% Exactly one output per cell – enforced by the harness
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

1 % ------------------------------------------------------------
2 %  ARC puzzle: rotate a plus‑sign component and use the maximal colour
3 % ------------------------------------------------------------
4 % ---- colour predicate (provided by the harness) ----
5 color(0..9).

7 input(Row,Col,Colour).

9 % rows and columns that appear in the input
10 max_row(R)     :- R = #max { R2 : input(R2,_,_) }.
11 max_col(C)     :- C = #max { C2 : input(_,C2,_) }.
12 % maximal colour in a concrete row / column
13 row_max(R,Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { C : input(R,_,C) }.
14 col_max(C,Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { R : input(_,C,R) }.
15 % a cell is a centre if it is the maximal colour of its row *and* column
16 center(Rc,RcC,M) :-
17     input(Rc,RcC,M),
18     row_max(Rc,Rmax),
19     col_max(RcC,Cmax),
20     M = Rmax,
21     M = Cmax.
22 % non‑zero cells that belong to the component of a centre (same row or column)
23 comp_cell(R,C,Rc,RcC) :- input(R,C,Col), Col != 0, R = Rc, C = RcC.
24 % component bounds for a centre (Rc,RcC)
25 comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin) :- RowMin = #min { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
26 comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
27 comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin) :- ColMin = #min { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.
28 comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.

31     comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),

38 
39 % maximal colour of a centre (already known from centre/3)
40 max_of(Rc,RcC,M) :- center(Rc,RcC,M).
41 % arms of a centre
42 in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
43     max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
44     comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
45     comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
46     comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
47     (R = Top, C >= ColMin, C <= ColMax).
48 
49 in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
50     max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
51     comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
52     comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax),
53     comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
54     (C = Centre, R >= Top, R <= RowMax - 1).
55 
56 % any cell that is on an arm (any maximal colour)
57 in_any_max_bar(R,C) :- in_max_bar(R,C,_).
58

63

65 output(R,C,M) :-
66     in_max_bar(R,C,M).
67 
68 % 2.  All other cells (including background) keep their original colour
69 output(R,C,V) :-

71     not in_any_max_bar(R,C).

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC puzzle: rotate a plus‑sign component and use the maximal colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- colour predicate (provided by the harness) ----
color(0..9).

% ---- input predicate (provided by the harness) ----
input(Row,Col,Colour).

% ---- auxiliary predicates ----

% rows and columns that appear in the input
max_row(R)     :- R = #max { R2 : input(R2,_,_) }.
max_col(C)     :- C = #max { C2 : input(_,C2,_) }.

% maximal colour in a concrete row / column
row_max(R,Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { C : input(R,_,C) }.
col_max(C,Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { R : input(_,C,R) }.

% a cell is a centre if it is the maximal colour of its row *and* column
center(Rc,RcC,M) :-
    input(Rc,RcC,M),
    row_max(Rc,Rmax),
    col_max(RcC,Cmax),
    M = Rmax,
    M = Cmax.

% non‑zero cells that belong to the component of a centre (same row or column)
comp_cell(R,C,Rc,RcC) :- input(R,C,Col), Col != 0, R = Rc, C = RcC.

% component bounds for a centre (Rc,RcC)
comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin) :- RowMin = #min { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin) :- ColMin = #min { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.

% derived data of a centre
comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top) :-
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    Top = RowMin - 1.
    % row just above the component

comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre) :-
    comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin),
    comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax),
    Centre = #max { X : 2*X <= ColMin + ColMax }.
    % floor((Cmin+Cmax)/2)

% maximal colour of a centre (already known from centre/3)
max_of(Rc,RcC,M) :- center(Rc,RcC,M).

% arms of a centre
in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    R = Top,
    C >= ColMin,
    C <= ColMax.

in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    C = Centre,
    R >= Top,
    R <= RowMax - 1.

% any cell that is on an arm (any maximal colour)
in_any_max_bar(R,C) :- in_max_bar(R,C,_).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   Output definition
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% 1.  Cells on an arm get the maximal colour of their component
output(R,C,M) :-
    in_max_bar(R,C,M).

% 2.  All other cells (including background) keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V),
    not in_any_max_bar(R,C).

% Exactly one output per cell – enforced by the harness
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC puzzle: rotate a plus‑sign component and use the maximal colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- colour predicate (provided by the harness) ----
color(0..9).

% ---- input predicate (provided by the harness) ----
input(Row,Col,Colour).

% ---- auxiliary predicates ----

% rows and columns that appear in the input
max_row(R)     :- R = #max { R2 : input(R2,_,_) }.
max_col(C)     :- C = #max { C2 : input(_,C2,_) }.

% maximal colour in a concrete row / column
row_max(R,Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { C : input(R,_,C) }.
col_max(C,Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { R : input(_,C,R) }.

% a cell is a centre if it is the maximal colour of its row *and* column
center(Rc,RcC,M) :-
    input(Rc,RcC,M),
    row_max(Rc,Rmax),
    col_max(RcC,Cmax),
    M = Rmax,
    M = Cmax.

% non‑zero cells that belong to the component of a centre (same row or column)
comp_cell(R,C,Rc,RcC) :- input(R,C,Col), Col != 0, R = Rc, C = RcC.

% component bounds for a centre (Rc,RcC)
comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin) :- RowMin = #min { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : comp_cell(R,_,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin) :- ColMin = #min { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.
comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : comp_cell(_,C,Rc,RcC) }.

% derived data of a centre
comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top) :-
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    Top = RowMin - 1.
    % row just above the component

comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre) :-
    comp_col_min(Rc,RcC,ColMin),
    comp_col_max(Rc,RcC,ColMax),
    Centre = #max { X : 2*X <= ColMin + ColMax }.
    % floor((Cmin+Cmax)/2)

% maximal colour of a centre (already known from centre/3)
max_of(Rc,RcC,M) :- center(Rc,RcC,M).

% arms of a centre
in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_row_min(Rc,RcC,RowMin),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    R = Top,
    C >= ColMin,
    C <= ColMax.

in_max_bar(R,C,M) :-
    max_of(Rc,RcC,M),
    comp_top(Rc,RcC,Top),
    comp_row_max(Rc,RcC,RowMax),
    comp_center_col(Rc,RcC,Centre),
    C = Centre,
    R >= Top,
    R <= RowMax - 1.

% any cell that is on an arm (any maximal colour)
in_any_max_bar(R,C) :- in_max_bar(R,C,_).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%   Output definition
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% 1.  Cells on an arm get the maximal colour of their component
output(R,C,M) :-
    in_max_bar(R,C,M).

% 2.  All other cells (including background) keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V),
    not in_any_max_bar(R,C).

% Exactly one output per cell – enforced by the harness
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

Pseudo code:

% Identify the smallest non-zero color (core)
color1(C1) :- C1 = #min { C : input(_,_,C), C != 0 }.

% Identify the next two distinct colors (layer colors)
color2(C2) :- C2 = #min { C : input(_,_,C), C != 0, C != C1 }.
color3(C3) :- C3 = #min { C : input(_,_,C), C != 0, C != C1, C != C2 }.

% Compute layer (Manhattan distance to a cell of core color)
layer(R,C,L) :- L = #min { L2 : input(Ri,Ci,C1), L2 = abs(Ri-R) + abs(Ci-C) }.

% Output based on layer (0 -> core, 1 -> next, 2 -> next)
output(R,C,C1) :- layer(R,C,0).
output(R,C,C2) :- layer(R,C,1).
output(R,C,C3) :- layer(R,C,2).

% Define output grid dimensions (same as input)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one output per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Ensure uniqueness per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Transformation rule: The output colour of a cell is determined by its Manhattan distance d to the nearest cell that has the smallest non‑zero colour (the core colour). Layer 0 (d=0) keeps the core colour, layer 1 takes the smallest colour that appears somewhere in the input and is different from the core, layer 2 takes the smallest colour that appears somewhere in the input and differs from the colours used for the previous two layers; if no such colour exists the next unused integer is taken. This produces concentric coloured shells around the core.

% Helper predicates
present_color(C) :- input(_,_,C).
c0(C0) :- C0 = #min { C : present_color(C), C != 0 }.

% Layer 1 colour choice
cnt1 = #count { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
zero1 :- cnt1 = 0.
c1_present = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
c1_global = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.

% Layer 2 colour choice (depends on colour used for layer 1)
c1a(C1a) :- C1a = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.  % colour that would be taken if present
c1b(C1b) :- C1b = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.           % fallback colour for layer 1
cnt2 = #count { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
zero2 :- cnt2 = 0.
c2a(C2a) :- C2a = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
c2b(C2b) :- C2b = #min { X : X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b, X != 0 }.

% Distance from the core colour
dist(R,C,D) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }.

% Cells that belong to a particular layer
layer0(R,C) :- dist(R,C,0).
layer1(R,C) :- dist(R,C,1).
layer2(R,C) :- dist(R,C,2).

% Apply the appropriate colour to each layer
output(R,C,V) :- layer0(R,C), not zero1, V = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
output(R,C,V) :- layer0(R,C), zero1, V = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.

output(R,C,V) :- layer1(R,C), not zero2, V = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
output(R,C,V) :- layer1(R,C), zero2, V = #min { X : X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b, X != 0 }.

% Grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Uniqueness
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
% For each cell we compute the Manhattan distance D to the nearest cell that
% carries the smallest non‑zero colour (the core colour).  D=0 => core colour,
% D=1 => the smallest colour that occurs somewhere in the input and is not the core,
% D=2 => the smallest colour that occurs somewhere in the input and differs from the
% colours already used for layers 0 and 1; if such a colour does not exist we take the
% smallest unused integer.  This creates concentric coloured shells around the core.
% ------------------------------

% core colour (smallest non‑zero colour in the input)
present_color(C) :- input(_,_,C).
c0(C0) :- C0 = #min { C : present_color(C), C != 0 }.

% ----- layer 1 colour (distance 1) -----
cnt1 = #count { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
zero1 :- cnt1 = 0.
c1a(C1a) :- C1a = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
c1b(C1b) :- C1b = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.

% ----- layer 2 colour (distance 2) -----
cnt2 = #count { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
zero2 :- cnt2 = 0.
c2a(C2a) :- C2a = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
c2b(C2b) :- C2b = #min { X : X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b, X != 0 }.

% ----- Manhattan distance to the core colour -----
dist0(R,C) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }, D = 0.
dist1(R,C) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }, D = 1.
dist2(R,C) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }, D = 2.

% cells belonging to a particular distance layer
layer0(R,C) :- dist0(R,C).
layer1(R,C) :- dist1(R,C).
layer2(R,C) :- dist2(R,C).

% colour for distance 0 (core colour)
output(R,C,V) :- layer0(R,C), not zero1, V = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
output(R,C,V) :- layer0(R,C), zero1,    V = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.

% colour for distance 1
output(R,C,V) :- layer1(R,C), not zero1, V = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
output(R,R,C,V) :- layer1(R,C), zero1,    V = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.

% colour for distance 2
output(R,C,V) :- layer2(R,C), not zero2, V = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
output(R,C,V) :- layer2(R,C), zero2,    V = #min { X : X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b, X != 0 }.

% ----- output grid dimensions (same as the input) -----
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Uniqueness (no two colours in the same cell)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

```asp
% -------- Transformation rule ----------
% The colour of a cell is determined by the Manhattan distance d to the nearest
% core cell (the smallest non‑zero colour in the input).  d=0 => core colour,
% d=1 => smallest colour that appears somewhere in the input and is not the core,
% d=2 => smallest colour that appears somewhere in the input and is different from
% the colours used for layers 0 and 1; if no such colour exists we take the smallest
% unused integer.  This yields concentric coloured shells around the core.

% -------- Helper predicates --------
present_color(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% core colour (smallest non‑zero colour)
c0(C0) :- C0 = #min { C : present_color(C), C != 0 }.

% -------- Layer 1 colour (distance 1) --------
cnt1 = #count { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
zero1 :- cnt1 = 0.
c1a(C1a) :- C1a = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
c1b(C1b) :- C1b = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.

% -------- Layer 2 colour (distance 2) --------
cnt2 = #count { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
zero2 :- cnt2 = 0.
c2a(C2a) :- C2a = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
c2b(C2b) :- C2b = #min { X : X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b, X != 0 }.

% -------- Manhattan distance to the core colour --------
dist0(R,C) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }, D = 0.
dist1(R,C) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }, D = 1.
dist2(R,C) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }, D = 2.

% -------- Cells belonging to a particular layer --------
layer0(R,C) :- dist0(R,C).
layer1(R,C) :- dist1(R,C).
layer2(R,C) :- dist2(R,C).

% -------- Output colour for each layer --------
% layer 0 (distance 0) – core colour
output(R,C,V) :- layer0(R,C), V = C0.

% layer 1 (distance 1)
output(R,C,V) :- layer1(R,C), not zero1, V = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
output(R,C,V) :- layer1(R,C), zero1,    V = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.

% layer 2 (distance 2)
output(R,C,V) :- layer2(R,C), not zero2, V = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
output(R,C,V) :- layer2(R,C), zero2,    V = #min { X : X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b, X != 0 }.

% -------- Output grid size (same as input) --------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% No two different colours in the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% For every cell we compute the Manhattan distance d to the nearest
% cell that carries the smallest non‑zero colour (the “core’’ colour).
%   d = 0  → the core colour itself.
%   d = 1  → the smallest colour that occurs somewhere in the input and
%            differs from the core colour.
%   d = 2  → the smallest colour that occurs somewhere in the input and
%            differs from the colours used for layers 0 and 1;
%            if no such colour exists we take the smallest unused integer.
% This yields concentric coloured shells around the core, exactly the
% pattern observed in the three training examples.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -------- Helper predicates --------
present_color(C)    :- input(_,_,C).                     % colours that really appear

% Core colour (smallest non‑zero colour in the input)
c0(C0)              :- C0 = #min { C : present_color(C), C != 0 }.

% -------- Layer 1 colour (distance = 1) --------
cnt1    = #count { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
zero1   :- cnt1 = 0.                                      % true iff there is no other colour
c1a(C1a) :- C1a = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
c1b(C1b) :- C1b = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.

% -------- Layer 2 colour (distance = 2) --------
cnt2    = #count { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
zero2   :- cnt2 = 0.                                      % true iff no colour is left for the outer shell
c2a(C2a) :- C2a = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
c2b(C2b) :- C2b = #min { X : X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b, X != 0 }.

% -------- Manhattan distance to the core colour --------
dist0(R,C) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }, D = 0.
dist1(R,C) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }, D = 1.
dist2(R,C) :- D = #min { D2 : input(Ri,Ci,c0), D2 = abs(Ri-R)+abs(Ci-C) }, D = 2.

% Cells that belong to a particular distance layer
layer0(R,C) :- dist0(R,C).
layer1(R,C) :- dist1(R,C).
layer2(R,C) :- dist2(R,C).

% -------- Output colour for each layer --------
% layer 0 (distance 0) – the core colour itself
output(R,C,V) :- layer0(R,C), V = C0.

% layer 1 (distance 1)
output(R,C,V) :- layer1(R,C), not zero1, V = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0 }.
output(R,C,V) :- layer1(R,C), zero1,    V = #min { X : X != c0, X != 0 }.

% layer 2 (distance 2)
output(R,C,V) :- layer2(R,C), not zero2, V = #min { X : present_color(X), X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b }.
output(R,C,V) :- layer2(R,C), zero2,    V = #min { X : X != c0, X != c1a, X != c1b, X != 0 }.

% -------- Output grid size (same as the input) --------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% No two different colours in the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
